If you are building LLM applications
that interact with vector databases, one
of the most crucial decisions you will
make is to how to break it down your tax
data into manageable pieces. This
process is called chunking and it
directly affects the quality, accuracy

strategies you can implement to optimize

Chunking is simply breaking down large
pieces of text into smaller segments
before embedding them and storing them
into a vector database. But why people
would do junk texts in the first place?
The main reason is to ensure we're
embedding pieces of context with minimal
noise and maintaining semantic
relevance. For example, in semantic
search applications, a proper chunking
ensures your search results accurately
capture the what users are looking for.
In conversational agents, good chunking
ensures the retrieved context applies to
the user's query and fits within the
token limitation of your LLM.
One very important aspect to consider
while you're embedding data is the
length of content being embedded because
it significantly affect the created
factors. Therefore, it is important to
talk about embedding short versus long
content. When embedding short content
like sentences, here's what you need to
consider.
Vectors, they focus on very specific
meanings.
They excel at precise matching. However,
it may miss broader context.
With longer context like paragraphs,
here's what you need to consider.
Embattings capture overall themes and
relationships.
It provides more comprehensive
representation.
However, it may delude the significancy
of specific details.
When it comes to size, here's a tip for
you. Your query length should ideally
match with your chunk size. Short
queries work better with sentence level
embeddings while longer, more complex
queries align better with paragraph
level embeddings.
Before choosing a chunking strategy,
these are the factors that you need to
consider. Number one, consider the
content nature. Are you working with
long documents like research papers? Or
perhaps you are working with short
textes like tweets.
Number two, consider the embedding model
characteristics. Different models
perform optionally with different chunk
sizes. For instance, sentence
transformer modules work well on
individual sentences. Meanwhile, text
embedding ADA 002 performs better with
256 or 512 token chunks. Number three,
consider the query expectations.
Will your users ask short, very specific
questions or perhaps they're asking more
complex ones?
Number four, also consider the
application purpose. Will you use the
retrieved results? You are going to use
them for semantic search, going to use
them for question answering, or you're
going to use them for summarization.
Answering these questions will guide you
toward the right chunking approach for
your use case.
Let's now talk about the chunking
approaches you can use in your
applications. Let's start with the fixed
size chunking. This is the most
straightforward approach. You simply
decide how many tokens each chunk should
contain and optionally you can add an
overlap between chunks to preserve
context.
Fixed side chunking is computationally
inexpensive and works well for most
common use cases.
Another approach is contextaware
chunking. This method takes advantage of
the natural structury of your content.
For example, you can implement sentence
level splitting. Many embedding models
are optimized for sentence level
content. On the application coding side,
frameworks like link chain provide
integrations with NLP libraries.
You can also leverage recursive
chunking. Recursive chunkers
intelligently divide text using a set of
separators until they reach the desired
chunk size. Another approach for
structured content like markdown or
latex, you can leverage specialized
splitters because of the uniqueness of
the structury of the content. This
approach is known as specialized
chunking.
Finally, you can implement semantic
chunking to identify natural topic
boundaries. This produces chunks that
maintain thematic coherence rather than
arbitrary token counts. As you can
imagine, this approach is a bit more
computationally expensive when compared
to the other approaches.
Now that we've discussed the chunking
approaches you can use, let's talk about
how you can decise the optional chunk
size. As you can imagine, this is going
to be one of the most important tasks
for your projects. To determine the best
chunk size for your application, you
should follow these three steps.
First pre-process your data by removing
all the noise and irrelevant content
from it. Second, you should select a
chunk sizes and a range of them to test
basin on your content type and your
embedding model. First, you have to try
smaller sizes like 128 until 256 tokens
to accommodate more granular
information. And perhaps you should try
larger sizes for 512 until 1,024 tokens
to accommodate more context.
And the third step is to evaluate the
performance. You should create
embeddings using different chunk sizes,
store them into a vector database, and
then run representative queries to
evaluate their performance. In this
evaluation, you should compare metrics
like relevancy, accuracy, and retrieval
speed.
While this process look tedious and if
you do it manually, you can automate all
of this by creating task and workflows
with frameworks like lingraph to handle
this evaluation process across different
chunking parameters.
To wrap up, chunking is a critical but

always remember.
There's no oneizefits-all solution.
